levels.title: The Repository Pattern
levels.description:
Separating Data Access from Business Logic

The repository pattern places all database operations behind a clean interface. Your services never write SQL directly; they call repository methods like find_by_id() or save().

Why?

  • Testability — swap the real repository for a mock in tests.
  • Single Responsibility — services hold business rules, repositories hold data access.
  • Consistency — transactions and error handling live in one place.

Architecture Layers

Controller / API
    ↓
Service        (@transactional)
    ↓
Repository     (@repository, @query)
    ↓
Database       (SQLAlchemy + async session)

How pico-sqlalchemy Helps

Instead of writing boilerplate session management and query code, you use decorators:

from pico_sqlalchemy import repository, query, transactional, AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column

@repository(entity=Product)
class ProductRepository:
    @query(expr="id = :id", unique=True)
    async def find_by_id(self, id: int) -> Product | None:
        ...  # body is never executed — the decorator builds the query

@component
class ProductService:
    def __init__(self, repo: ProductRepository):
        self._repo = repo

    @transactional(read_only=True)
    async def get_product(self, id: int) -> Product | None:
        return await self._repo.find_by_id(id)

In the following labs you will build each layer step by step.

levels.title: Defining Entities with AppBase
levels.description:
SQLAlchemy 2.0 Entity Definitions

Every database table is represented by an entity class that inherits from AppBase. Columns are declared with Mapped[T] type annotations and mapped_column().

from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String

class User(AppBase):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))

The Mapped[int] annotation tells SQLAlchemy the column type. Without it, the column won't be recognised.

Task: Open models.py. The Product entity is missing its Mapped type annotations on the name and price columns. Add them so the test passes.

files.name: models.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String


class Product(AppBase):
    __tablename__ = "products"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    # Fix: add Mapped[str] annotation
    name = mapped_column(String(100))
    # Fix: add Mapped[float] annotation
    price = mapped_column()

files.name: test_models.py
files.content:
from models import Product
from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase


def test_product_inherits_appbase():
    assert issubclass(Product, AppBase)


def test_product_has_tablename():
    assert Product.__tablename__ == "products"


def test_product_columns_exist():
    col_names = [c.name for c in Product.__table__.columns]
    assert "id" in col_names
    assert "name" in col_names
    assert "price" in col_names


def test_column_annotations():
    annotations = Product.__annotations__
    assert "name" in annotations, "name must have a Mapped type annotation"
    assert "price" in annotations, "price must have a Mapped type annotation"

hints: Each column needs a type annotation like `name: Mapped[str]` before the `= mapped_column(...)` part.
The `price` column stores a decimal number. Use `Mapped[float]` as the type annotation.
The final lines should look like `name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))` and `price: Mapped[float] = mapped_column()`.

solution.models.py:
from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String


class Product(AppBase):
    __tablename__ = "products"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))
    price: Mapped[float] = mapped_column()

levels.title: The @repository Decorator
levels.description:
Turning a Class into a Repository

The @repository decorator does three things:

  1. Stores metadata (including the entity) on the class.
  2. Wraps every public async method with an implicit transaction.
  3. Registers the class as a pico-ioc @component.
from pico_sqlalchemy import repository

@repository(entity=User)
class UserRepository:
    async def save(self, user: User) -> User:
        ...

Task: Open repositories.py. The ProductRepository class is missing the @repository decorator. Add @repository(entity=Product) so the test passes.

files.name: models.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String


class Product(AppBase):
    __tablename__ = "products"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))
    price: Mapped[float] = mapped_column()

files.name: repositories.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy import repository, get_session, SessionManager
from models import Product


# Add the @repository decorator with entity=Product
class ProductRepository:
    def __init__(self, sm: SessionManager):
        self._sm = sm

    async def save(self, product: Product) -> Product:
        session = get_session(self._sm)
        session.add(product)
        return product

    async def delete(self, product: Product) -> None:
        session = get_session(self._sm)
        await session.delete(product)

files.name: test_repositories.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy.decorators import REPOSITORY_META
from repositories import ProductRepository
from models import Product


def test_repository_has_metadata():
    meta = getattr(ProductRepository, REPOSITORY_META, None)
    assert meta is not None, "ProductRepository must have @repository metadata"


def test_repository_entity_is_product():
    meta = getattr(ProductRepository, REPOSITORY_META)
    assert meta.get("entity") is Product, "@repository must specify entity=Product"

hints: Import `repository` from `pico_sqlalchemy` (already imported at the top of the file).
Place `@repository(entity=Product)` directly above the `class ProductRepository:` line.
The decorator both registers the component and stores metadata. No other changes needed.

solution.repositories.py:
from pico_sqlalchemy import repository, get_session, SessionManager
from models import Product


@repository(entity=Product)
class ProductRepository:
    def __init__(self, sm: SessionManager):
        self._sm = sm

    async def save(self, product: Product) -> Product:
        session = get_session(self._sm)
        session.add(product)
        return product

    async def delete(self, product: Product) -> None:
        session = get_session(self._sm)
        await session.delete(product)

levels.title: Declarative @query
levels.description:
Replacing Method Bodies with Queries

The @query decorator replaces a method's body with an auto-generated SQL query. You provide a WHERE expression and the interceptor does the rest.

@query(expr="email = :email", unique=True)
async def find_by_email(self, email: str) -> User | None:
    ...  # body is never executed

Key options:

  • expr — a WHERE clause with :param placeholders.
  • unique=True — return a single row (or None).
  • paged=True — return a Page instead of a list.

Task: Open repositories.py. Add @query decorators to find_by_id (with unique=True) and find_all (no unique). Check the method signatures for the correct expr.

files.name: models.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String


class Product(AppBase):
    __tablename__ = "products"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))
    price: Mapped[float] = mapped_column()

files.name: repositories.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy import repository, query
from models import Product
from typing import Optional


@repository(entity=Product)
class ProductRepository:

    # Add @query decorator: find one product by id
    async def find_by_id(self, id: int) -> Optional[Product]:
        ...

    # Add @query decorator: find all products
    async def find_all(self) -> list[Product]:
        ...

files.name: test_queries.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy.decorators import QUERY_META
from repositories import ProductRepository


def test_find_by_id_has_query_meta():
    method = ProductRepository.find_by_id
    meta = getattr(method, QUERY_META, None)
    assert meta is not None, "find_by_id must have @query metadata"


def test_find_by_id_is_unique():
    meta = getattr(ProductRepository.find_by_id, QUERY_META)
    assert meta["unique"] is True, "find_by_id should use unique=True"


def test_find_by_id_expr():
    meta = getattr(ProductRepository.find_by_id, QUERY_META)
    assert meta["expr"] == "id = :id", "find_by_id expr should be 'id = :id'"


def test_find_all_has_query_meta():
    method = ProductRepository.find_all
    meta = getattr(method, QUERY_META, None)
    assert meta is not None, "find_all must have @query metadata"


def test_find_all_is_not_unique():
    meta = getattr(ProductRepository.find_all, QUERY_META)
    assert meta["unique"] is False, "find_all should not use unique=True"

hints: The `@query` decorator goes above the method definition, just like any other decorator.
For `find_by_id`, the parameter is named `id`, so the expression is `"id = :id"`. Add `unique=True` since it returns one product.
For `find_all`, use an expression that matches everything. Try `expr="1 = 1"` with no `unique` argument (it defaults to `False`).

levels.title: @transactional in Services
levels.description:
Declarative Transaction Management

The @transactional decorator wraps a method in a database transaction. It supports several options:

@transactional                        # read-write, REQUIRED propagation
@transactional(read_only=True)        # read-only (no commit)
@transactional(propagation="REQUIRES_NEW")  # new transaction

Use read_only=True on methods that only read data. This skips the commit step and signals your intent clearly.

Task: Open services.py. Add @transactional to:

  • get_product and list_products — use read_only=True
  • create_product — use the default (read-write)

files.name: models.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy import AppBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String


class Product(AppBase):
    __tablename__ = "products"

    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    name: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100))
    price: Mapped[float] = mapped_column()

files.name: repositories.py
services.py
test_services.py
files.content:
from pico_sqlalchemy.decorators import TRANSACTIONAL_META
from services import ProductService


def test_get_product_is_transactional():
    meta = getattr(ProductService.get_product, TRANSACTIONAL_META, None)
    assert meta is not None, "get_product must have @transactional metadata"


def test_get_product_is_read_only():
    meta = getattr(ProductService.get_product, TRANSACTIONAL_META)
    assert meta["read_only"] is True, "get_product should be read_only=True"


def test_list_products_is_read_only():
    meta = getattr(ProductService.list_products, TRANSACTIONAL_META)
    assert meta is not None, "list_products must have @transactional metadata"
    assert meta["read_only"] is True, "list_products should be read_only=True"


def test_create_product_is_read_write():
    meta = getattr(ProductService.create_product, TRANSACTIONAL_META)
    assert meta is not None, "create_product must have @transactional metadata"
    assert meta["read_only"] is False, "create_product should be read-write"

hints: Import `transactional` from `pico_sqlalchemy` (already imported at the top).
For read methods, use `@transactional(read_only=True)` above the method definition.
For `create_product`, use `@transactional` without arguments (defaults to read-write).

levels.title: Pagination with PageRequest
levels.description:
Paginating Query Results

When a table has thousands of rows, returning them all at once is impractical. pico-sqlalchemy provides three types for pagination:

  • PageRequest — specifies which page to fetch and the page size.
  • Page[T] — a frozen result containing the items and total count.
  • Sort — specifies column ordering.

Creating a PageRequest

from pico_sqlalchemy import PageRequest, Sort

# First page, 20 items, sorted by name ascending
req = PageRequest(page=0, size=20, sorts=[Sort("name")])

# Third page, 10 items, sorted by price descending
req2 = PageRequest(page=2, size=10, sorts=[Sort("price", "DESC")])

Using @query with Pagination

Add paged=True to the @query decorator and accept a page: PageRequest parameter:

@repository(entity=Product)
class ProductRepository:

    @query(expr="1 = 1", paged=True)
    async def find_all(self, page: PageRequest) -> Page[Product]:
        ...  # body replaced by interceptor

Reading a Page Result

page = await repo.find_all(PageRequest(page=0, size=10))

print(page.content)          # list of Product objects
print(page.total_elements)   # total matching rows
print(page.total_pages)      # computed ceiling division
print(page.is_first)         # True if page 0
print(page.is_last)          # True if last page

Sorting Without Pagination

Sort can also be used independently with @query methods by passing it as a parameter. The interceptor applies ORDER BY clauses automatically.

Pagination keeps APIs responsive and memory usage predictable — always use it for list endpoints in production.
